abstract: Distributional semantic models derive computational representations of word meaning by analyzing patterns of lexical co-occurrence within large text corpora. While these models have become a cornerstone of computational linguistics—offering reliable estimates of semantic relatedness across various processing tasks—they rely exclusively on linguistic input. This reliance on text constitutes a significantly impoverished foundation compared to the multisensory perceptual streams that ground human semantic knowledge. To address this lack of grounding, we leverage computer vision techniques that automatically identify discrete "visual words" within images. By doing so, we extend the distributional representation of a concept to encompass its co-occurrence with visual features in its associated imagery. We propose a flexible multimodal architecture designed to integrate textual and visual distributional information. Our empirical evaluations demonstrate that this integrated model consistently outperforms purely text-based approaches, capturing complementary semantic nuances that are absent from linguistic data alone.
